lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market beauty-salon firms providing repeat hair cutting, styling, coloring, shampooing, facial, makeup, and related cosmetology services directly to external customers, including multi-location and franchised operators when the screened firm operates the service locations.
lens.excluded_activities: Independent booth renters outside the screened firm's operations, booth-rental-only landlords, nail-only salons, cosmetology schools, permanent-makeup specialists classified elsewhere, product-only retailers, passive franchisors or trademark owners, shell entities, and captive internal services.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers purchase appointments or walk-in beauty services at posted or quoted per-service prices and often return on a maintenance cycle; operators may also receive product sales and booth or chair rent. Revenue is repeat-oriented but generally transactional rather than contractual.

a.bridge: BLS reports employment and wages for the broader Personal Care Services industry, while O*NET directly lists hands-on beauty work alongside scheduling, customer records, payments, sales, inventory, training, and administrative activities for hairdressers and cosmetologists. I translate the occupation mix and task list into a wage-weighted exposed-task range for beauty salons. The broader industry includes other personal-care models and may overstate dedicated reception, while payroll data omit owner and contractor administrative work that may increase exposure in salons.
a.rationale: Cutting, coloring, styling, sanitation, assessment, and close-contact service dominate labor and remain manual and interpersonal. AI exposure is concentrated in reception, scheduling, reminders, customer records, marketing and consultation support, payments, purchasing, basic finance, workforce coordination, and parts of management.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-payroll study of multi-location beauty salons that separates service delivery, reception, consultation, marketing, scheduling, records, finance, inventory, and management—and identifies tasks already automated—would materially change the range.
caveats: The BLS occupation mix is for NAICS 812100 and includes barber shops, nail salons, spas, massage businesses, and other personal care services.
Employer data omit self-employed and contractor stylists and may misstate the wage mix of scaled salons.
Task exposure is not whole-job displacement and excludes existing booking and point-of-sale automation.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures whether U.S. businesses use AI in any business function and near-term expected use, not the five-year implemented share of exposed beauty-salon tasks. I use recent national adoption as an anchor and raise the central five-year conversion because the exposed workflows are standard scheduling, messaging, records, marketing, merchandising, and office tasks increasingly delivered through packaged software. This may overstate implementation by fragmented salons with contractor-heavy operations and understate it for scaled multi-location firms with centralized systems.
rho.rationale: Beauty salons have several digitally mediated workflows around a physical service, making implementation more accessible than automation of the treatment itself. Five years allows packaged tools to diffuse, but fragmented data, stylist autonomy, customer nuance, integration limits, and change capacity prevent full conversion of exposed work.
rho.change_evidence: A survey of multi-location salon operators measuring production AI coverage by workflow, stylist adoption, override rates, software integration, customer response, and realized labor hours would materially change the range.
caveats: BTOS covers all nonfarm businesses and measures any AI use rather than task implementation.
No published five-year AI adoption cohort specific to beauty salons was identified.
Some apparent benefit may be conventional workflow automation already deployed and therefore outside current not-yet-automated exposure.

e.rationale: Most scaled beauty-salon firms directly sell recurring or repeat services to external customers and fit the operating-service lens. The discount allows for booth-rental-only economics, passive franchising, product-heavy firms, entities lacking operational independence, and classification or margin-bridge errors across a varied service mix.
e.change_evidence: A record-level census of firms in the EBITDA band showing operating locations, service-line revenue, booth-rent share, franchise role, contractor structure, owner labor, and normalized earnings would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: Beauty-salon receipts can include booth rent and retail products alongside services.
Some firms combine beauty, barber, facial, spa, or other activities across establishments and may have a different primary code.
The supplied firm-count estimate is margin-bridged and sensitive to contractor accounting, owner compensation, and multi-establishment firm structure.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sell-or-transfer plans among U.S. employer-business owners, while BizBuySell reports completed transactions for a combined hair-salon and barber-shop marketplace. I discount the broad plan rate for noncompletion, closures, gifts without transferable operations, and internal reorganizations, while retaining a material probability because completed category transactions are observed. Marketplace evidence overrepresents firms offered for sale and is mostly below the target earnings band, while larger multi-location salons may be more transferable than typical owner-operated listings.
s5.rationale: Employer-business owners report meaningful five-year transfer intent, and the active salon-barber transaction market shows that customer, lease, staff, and operating assets can transfer. Stylist and owner dependence still reduces the probability that a contemplated exit becomes a qualifying continuing control transfer.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year PitchBook, BizBuySell, Axial, franchise-resale, and broker-deal census matched to the identified LMM salon cohort, with control terms, seller role, closure status, and post-close continuity, would produce a direct rate.
caveats: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries rather than beauty salons.
BizBuySell combines hair salons and barber shops and provides no eligible-firm denominator.
Marketplace transactions skew much smaller than the frozen EBITDA band and may have greater owner dependence.

q.rationale: Posted or quoted per-service pricing has no automatic cost-plus pass-through, so savings and utilization gains in reception, scheduling, marketing, records, and administration can initially remain with the operator. Over five years, local competition, stylist compensation, software fees, promotions, customer experience reinvestment, and service-price adjustments share part of the benefit. Contractor and booth-rental structures can shift value away from the firm operating the location.
q.change_evidence: Location-level contribution-margin bridges before and after workflow implementation, including prices, service mix, labor schedules, commissions, booth terms, software cost, cancellations, promotions, retention, and competitor response, would materially change the range.
caveats: No longitudinal study of AI-driven beauty-salon gross benefit retention was identified.
Employee, commission, contractor, and booth-rental models allocate benefits differently.
The range applies only after implementation and does not include demand volume or implementation difficulty.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment growth for hairdressers, hairstylists, and cosmetologists over 2024–2034, not constant-price, constant-quality beauty-service quantity over five years. I convert roughly half of the decade growth into a five-year demand anchor and widen around it for productivity, self-employment, visit frequency, population, real income, and service-mix effects. Employment growth could understate demand if productivity rises or overstate it if services become more labor intensive.
d5.rationale: Hair maintenance, coloring, styling, and related beauty services generate repeat visits, and BLS expects occupational growth rather than contraction. Demand remains discretionary and exposed to household budgets, at-home products, style cycles, and visit-frequency changes, so the lower bound allows mild real volume decline.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of constant-price transaction counts from major salon booking and payment platforms, stratified by same-store locations and service type, plus a forward customer-frequency survey, would materially change the range.
caveats: Employment is an input measure rather than service quantity.
BLS occupation projections span work outside beauty salons and include self-employment.
The horizon conversion assumes the projected decade path is approximately smooth.

o.rationale: The current basket centers on cutting, coloring, styling, scalp and facial work, sanitation, and aesthetic judgment delivered physically to a customer. Software can support visualization, recommendation, booking, records, and communication but cannot plausibly replace the accountable trained operator for most service quantity within five years. A small residual allows at-home care, self-service, and limited equipment-assisted substitution, especially for consultation and makeup-related activity.
o.change_evidence: Customer trials and commercial deployment data for autonomous, robotic, or self-service hair and beauty systems, including safety, licensing, insurance, completion quality, repeat use, and unit economics, would materially change the range.
caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than an observed substitution rate.
Operator requirement varies across haircuts, coloring, facials, makeup, consultation, ancillary retail, and other salon services.
State licensing and safety rules can change over the horizon.

narrative.executive_view: Beauty salons are repeat, local, externally paid service businesses with a physical and relationship-intensive core. AI can materially improve the surrounding reception, scheduling, records, marketing, merchandising, and administrative workflow, but only a minority of wage-weighted work is exposed because customers buy skilled hands-on execution. Transfer evidence exists for the combined salon-barber market, though direct rates for scaled LMM salons are unavailable.
narrative.ai_impact: Packaged tools can handle appointment requests, reminders, routine messages, marketing drafts, customer-record summaries, style-visualization support, basic analytics, inventory prompts, and portions of bookkeeping. Cutting, coloring, styling, treatment, sanitation, live assessment, exception handling, and the trusted customer relationship remain human-delivered.
narrative.value_capture: Per-service pricing permits some administrative savings and utilization improvement to remain with the operator because there is no contractual pass-through. Competition, stylist compensation, software costs, promotions, and reinvestment in experience share the benefit over time, with contractor and booth-rental models altering where value accrues.
narrative.firm_availability: Most scaled operating beauty salons fit the lens, and the frozen firm population is more substantial than for barber shops. BizBuySell confirms an active combined transaction market, but typical sold firms are much smaller than the target band and stylist or owner dependence can impair transferability.
narrative.demand_durability: Repeat hair and beauty maintenance remains physically delivered, and BLS projects growth for hairdressers, hairstylists, and cosmetologists. Real volume can fluctuate with disposable income, at-home alternatives, service frequency, and style trends, while software substitution is concentrated in the surrounding workflow rather than the treatment itself.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key uncertainties are contractor and booth-rental economics, self-employment omitted from employer occupation data, stylist-dependent customer relationships, mixed service lines, and broader-industry proxies. Implementation may displace little payroll if automation merely saves unpaid owner time or if reception labor is already minimized.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat local demand and physically operator-required services preserve the core customer relationship while AI can streamline several surrounding workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most labor remains hands-on and relationship-specific, limiting the wage-weighted exposure available for implementation.

sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Form OS-81210: Personal Care Services
sources.supports: Identifies beauty salons, unisex shops, day spas with hair services, facial salons, and barber shops as classified activities and lists hair, skin, retail, and booth-rental receipt categories.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 812100
sources.supports: Provides the broader personal-care occupation and wage mix, including hairdressers, personal appearance workers, management, reception, and administrative employment used for the exposure proxy.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Hairdressers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists
sources.supports: Lists hands-on cutting, coloring, styling, sanitation, assessment, appointment, records, payment, sales, supply, training, and administrative tasks and documents continual manual and close-contact work.

sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: Reports recent U.S. business use and near-term expected use of AI by firm size, used as a broad implementation anchor.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports five-year sell-or-transfer plans among U.S. business owners, including a higher rate for employer-business owners, used as a broad transfer-intent proxy.

sources.title: BizBuySell Insight Report Data Tables: Full-Year 2025
sources.supports: Reports completed hair-salon and barber-shop marketplace transactions and transaction characteristics, confirming an active but combined and mostly small-business transfer market.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Barbers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists
sources.supports: Describes duties, licensing, work setting, and the 2024–2034 employment outlook, including the hairdresser, hairstylist, and cosmetologist projection used for the demand proxy.
